name: Hermes Agent (Nous Research)
description: Hermes Agent is an open-source, self-improving AI agent by Nous Research. Launched Feb 2026, 64K+ stars in 2 months. The ONLY framework with a closed learning loop: Observe → Plan → Act → Learn. After complex tasks (5+ tool calls), the agent synthesizes reusable skill documents (agentskills.io standard). Three-layer memory: short-term context, episodic (past experiences), procedural (skills/workflows). Pluggable memory backends (Honcho, Mem0, Vectorize Hindsight, RetainDB). Cache-aware: freezes system prompt at session init so learning doesn't grow token bills. Benchmarked: agents with self-created skills complete tasks 40% faster than fresh instances.
oneLiner: Open-source self-improving AI agent — gets smarter the longer it runs, deploys anywhere, speaks every platform.
funding: $70M+ (crypto-priced token rounds, used for compute reserves and team expansion). MiniMax AI partnership.
pricing: MIT licensed, free. Hosting: $5/mo VPS. API costs: $15-80/mo typical. Zero with Ollama local models.
capabilities: Closed learning loop: Observe → Plan → Act → Learn — only framework with this
Autonomous skill creation after 5+ tool-call tasks (agentskills.io standard)
Skills self-improve during use — measurably 40% faster than fresh instances
Three-layer memory: short-term context, episodic experiences, procedural skills
Pluggable memory backends: Honcho, Mem0, Vectorize Hindsight, RetainDB
Cache-aware architecture — learning doesn't grow token costs
15+ messaging platforms: Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal, Matrix, Email, SMS, CLI + more
200+ models via OpenRouter, Nous Portal, OpenAI, or custom endpoints
MCP support for extended tool capabilities
Built-in cron scheduler — natural language scheduling for unattended automation
Browser Use integration for web automation (v0.8.0)
Plugin system with CLI subcommands, API hooks, session lifecycle events
FTS5 cross-session search with LLM summarization
Honcho dialectic user modeling for personalization
$5/mo VPS or free with local models — lowest cost in market

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: CLI-first: install via pip/Docker, configure YAML, run 'hermes' command. Multi-platform gateway connects to messaging apps automatically. Skills and memory develop autonomously during use. Plugin system for extensibility.
strengths: Self-improving is genuinely novel — agent gets better without user intervention
64K+ GitHub stars in 2 months — fastest-growing agent project of 2026
Multi-platform out of the box — one agent, 15+ messaging platforms
Cost-optimized — runs on $5/mo VPS, or free with local models
MCP support + plugin system — highly extensible
MIT licensed — full freedom, no vendor lock-in
weaknesses: No visual builder or web UI for agent creation
Self-improving can be unpredictable — hard to audit autonomous skill changes
Crypto-funded raises governance questions for enterprise buyers
No enterprise support SLA or compliance certifications
Plugin ecosystem is early — limited community plugins
notableFeatures: Autonomous skill creation — agent builds new capabilities from complex tasks
Agent-curated memory with periodic nudges — growing knowledge base
Cross-session recall via FTS5 search + LLM summarization
Built-in cron scheduler for unattended automation
Platform-agnostic deployment — $5 VPS to GPU clusters

recentDevelopments: 2026-03: MiniMax AI partnership announced — multimodal expansion (vision, audio)
2026-04-08: v0.8.0 (v2026.4.8) — 209 merged PRs, Browser Use, remote backends, worktree parallelism
2026-04: 64,200+ GitHub stars — fastest-growing agent project of 2026
2026-04: Plugin system expansion — CLI subcommands, API hooks, session lifecycle events
2026-04: Matrix Tier 1 support, Discord channel controls, Signal media delivery, Mattermost attachments

deploymentOptions.notes: $5 VPS, GPU cluster, serverless, or local. Docker and pip install supported.

subCriteria.name: Agent Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: CLI/YAML only — no visual builder. 'hermes model' command for model switching is clean but developer-only.

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Value
subCriteria.rationale: pip install + YAML config + run. Fast for developers. Self-improving means value compounds over time.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Centralized logging, session search, config validation. No dedicated debugging UI.

subCriteria.name: Documentation & Learning
subCriteria.rationale: Growing docs site, DeepWiki coverage, active GitHub community. Still maturing.

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: Single agent with autonomous skill creation. Not multi-agent orchestration in the CrewAI/LangGraph sense.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop Design
subCriteria.rationale: Conversational interface via messaging platforms enables natural HITL interaction.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: Built-in cron scheduler, plugin system, MCP tools. Rich automation patterns.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: Multi-platform gateway means the agent is accessible to the whole team via their preferred channel.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: 15+ messaging platforms out of the box — broadest platform support in any agent framework.

subCriteria.name: API & Protocol Standards
subCriteria.rationale: MCP support, OpenRouter (200+ models), plugin API hooks, webhook triggers.

subCriteria.name: Authentication & Access
subCriteria.rationale: Platform-native auth per channel. No enterprise SSO.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline & RAG
subCriteria.rationale: FTS5 session search, agent-curated memory, web extraction tools. Growing knowledge base.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Kill Switches
subCriteria.rationale: Self-improving nature makes guardrails critical but they're minimal. Autonomous skill creation is a governance risk.

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail & Compliance
subCriteria.rationale: Centralized logging exists. No compliance certifications. Crypto funding raises enterprise governance questions.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Access Control
subCriteria.rationale: Platform-level channel controls (Discord channels). No granular RBAC.

subCriteria.name: Explainability & Transparency
subCriteria.rationale: Session search helps but autonomous skill changes are hard to audit.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: $5 VPS to GPU clusters to serverless. Docker, pip, local models. Maximum flexibility.

subCriteria.name: Scaling & Monitoring
subCriteria.rationale: Centralized logging. Worktree parallelism in v0.8.0. No horizontal scaling dashboard.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: $5/mo hosting + $15-80/mo API. Zero with local models. Very cost-effective.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem Maturity
subCriteria.rationale: 64K stars but only 2 months old. Plugin ecosystem early. Community growing fast.

scoringRationale: 62/100 overall. Strongest: Integration (16/20). Weakest: Governance (8/20). Breakdown: Creation (12/20), Orchestration (14/20), Integration (16/20), Governance (8/20), Operations (12/20).
targetUser: Developers and power users who want a personal AI agent that gets smarter over time across all their messaging platforms

communityBuzz: Fastest-growing AI agent project of 2026 — 64K stars in 2 months
Self-improving narrative resonates strongly with developer community
MiniMax partnership signals multimodal ambitions (vision, audio, robotics)
Crypto funding model is divisive — loved by Web3, questioned by enterprise
Compared favorably to OpenClaw (Manus) but with open-source advantage

coreCompetence.bestAt: The ONLY agent framework with a closed learning loop — Observe, Plan, Act, Learn. Agents create reusable skills from experience (40% faster after learning). Three-layer memory (context, episodic, procedural) with pluggable backends. Cache-aware so learning doesn't increase costs.
coreCompetence.targetUser: Developers who want a personal AI agent that genuinely gets smarter over time — not just retrieves memory, but creates new capabilities from experience.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins uniquely on self-improvement (no competitor has a closed learning loop). Loses to OpenClaw on breadth (345K stars, multi-agent, larger ecosystem). Loses to CrewAI/LangGraph on enterprise orchestration. Different category: Hermes is a single self-improving agent, not a multi-agent platform. The question isn't Hermes vs CrewAI — it's whether your agents should learn from experience.

walkthrough.title: Hermes Agent: PM & CTO Walkthrough — Self-Improving AI Agent

pmVerdict.summary: Hermes Agent introduces the most important new pattern in the agent space: the closed learning loop. Observe → Plan → Act → Learn means the agent creates reusable skills from experience — measurably 40% faster after learning. Three-layer memory (context, episodic, procedural) with pluggable backends (Honcho, Mem0, etc.) is architecturally sound. 15+ platforms and $5/mo hosting make evaluation effortless. But: self-improving is also self-modifying — governance nightmare for enterprise. Crypto funding, no compliance certs, 2 months old. This is the pattern every agent factory will eventually copy — but Hermes itself is for experimenters, not production enterprise.
strengths: Closed learning loop is genuinely novel — Observe/Plan/Act/Learn creates compound value
40% faster task completion with self-created skills — benchmarked, not just claimed
Pluggable memory backends (Honcho, Mem0, Vectorize, RetainDB) — no lock-in on memory layer
Cache-aware architecture — learning doesn't grow token costs, a real engineering achievement
64K stars in 2 months + 209 merged PRs in v0.8.0 — fastest development velocity in the space
risks: Self-improving = self-modifying — who audits autonomous skill changes?
Single agent, not multi-agent — doesn't compete with orchestration platforms
Crypto funding raises procurement questions for enterprise buyers
2 months old — zero production case studies at scale
Skill quality varies — autonomous creation doesn't guarantee autonomous quality

ctoVerdict.summary: Hermes Agent's closed learning loop is the architecture every agent platform will eventually adopt — agents that create reusable skills from experience, with measurable 40% performance improvement. The three-layer memory with pluggable backends is well-designed. But deploying self-improving agents in enterprise is a governance unsolved problem. Who approves autonomous skill creation? How do you audit procedural memory changes? Until these questions have answers, Hermes is a research project, not a production platform. The pattern, however, should be stolen immediately.
ctoVerdict.decision: STUDY — Steal the Pattern, Not the Product
strengths: Closed learning loop is the most important architectural innovation in 2026 agents
Cache-aware memory — learning efficiency without cost growth is genuinely hard to build
Pluggable memory backends prevent storage lock-in
agentskills.io standard for skill documents — potential industry standard
MIT licensed — we can study and adopt the pattern without using the product
dealBreakers: Self-improving agents are fundamentally ungovernable today
Crypto-funded — token-based financing model is foreign to enterprise procurement
Single agent focus — not an orchestration platform
Autonomous skill creation without approval gates is a compliance violation in regulated industries

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: CLI installation and configuration shown in tutorials
scoringAlignment.gap: No visual builder — creation is YAML/CLI only

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Cron scheduler, plugin system, autonomous skills described and demonstrated
scoringAlignment.gap: Not multi-agent orchestration — single agent with growing capabilities

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: 15+ platforms, MCP support, 200+ models all documented and demonstrated
scoringAlignment.gap: Integration breadth is the core strength — well verified

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Logging exists but self-improving nature creates fundamental audit challenges
scoringAlignment.gap: Governance is the Achilles heel — autonomous changes hard to control

steps.title: CLI with Tools and Skills
steps.description: Terminal showing hermes v0.4.0 — Available Tools (browser, execute_code, search, patch, read_file) and 30+ Available Skills (apple-notes, claude-code, github-auth, obsidian, etc.).

works: 30+ skills out of the box — apple-notes, claude-code, github-auth, obsidian. Massive breadth.
Tool categories clearly organized — browser, code execution, file operations, search
CLI aesthetic with retro Hermes banner — strong brand identity for developer community
doesntWork: Wall of text in terminal — overwhelming for first-time users
No visual skill browser or searchable catalog
Skill names are cryptic — what does pokemon-player do in an agent framework?
pmCritique.userPsychology: The terminal output is both the strength and weakness — developers see power, non-developers see complexity.

ctoCritique.verdict: 30+ built-in skills show ecosystem maturity for a 2-month-old project. But no governance over which skills are active.
concerns: No skill approval workflow — all 30+ active by default
Skill quality varies — community-contributed vs core team
No skill sandboxing — each skill has full agent permissions

steps.title: Natural Language Cron Jobs
steps.description: User types: Every morning at 9am, pull top 5 HN stories, summarize, score. Agent finds hn-audio-briefing skill and sets up the cron job. Claude Opus 4.6 as model.

works: Natural language to cron job — no crontab syntax needed
Agent discovers the right skill autonomously — hn-audio-briefing matched to request
Claude Opus 4.6 shown — uses frontier models for reasoning
doesntWork: Skill synthesizing takes time (loading indicator) — latency for autonomous discovery
No confirmation before setting up cron — agent just does it
Cost of running daily cron jobs not shown
pmCritique.userPsychology: The cron demo is the killer pitch — describe what you want, agent sets it up forever. Personal AI assistant promise made real.

ctoCritique.verdict: Natural language scheduling is compelling but autonomous cron creation without approval is a runaway-cost risk.
concerns: No cost estimation before cron creation
No approval gate — agent creates scheduled jobs unilaterally
Cron jobs run against API credits with no budget cap

steps.title: Architecture Comparison Detail
steps.description: Purpose, Interface, Scope, Strengths side by side. Hermes: self-improving skills on the fly. OpenClaw: more stable, larger community building.

works: Self-improving as Interface differentiator is accurate
Built for Open Models is strong open-source positioning
doesntWork: Interface comparison oversimplifies — both have CLIs and APIs
Scope: tinkerers vs enterprise is reductive
pmCritique.userPsychology: This crystallizes the decision: self-improving for hackers, or stable for production. Most CTOs choose stability.

ctoCritique.verdict: Confirms Hermes is the research/experiment choice. Production deployments need the stability OpenClaw offers.
concerns: Self-improving interface is ungovernable by enterprise standards
Tinkerer scope means limited enterprise use cases
Community building focus suggests enterprise is secondary

steps.title: Running in Discord with Sub-Agents
steps.description: Split view: Discord channel with Hermes resolving configs and spawning sub-agents + Terminal with coder gateway, Claude Opus sessions, resource monitoring.

works: Discord integration is native — agent responds in-channel like a team member
Agent shows reasoning — resolution chain for config lookup is transparent
Sub-agent spawning visible — creates child agents for specific tasks
doesntWork: Dense technical output in Discord — not user-friendly for non-developers
Terminal view shows raw process output — no dashboard
Multi-agent spawning via Discord is powerful but chaotic
pmCritique.userPsychology: The Discord split view is the most realistic demo — this is daily life with Hermes. Chatbot that runs code and spawns agents from your Discord.

ctoCritique.verdict: Discord as the control plane for AI agents is novel but raises serious enterprise concerns about data exposure in chat channels.
concerns: Sensitive operations visible in Discord channels — data exposure risk
Sub-agent spawning without approval creates resource sprawl
Terminal process output mixed with chat creates audit challenges
enterpriseGaps: No data classification for Discord messages
No sub-agent budget or permission controls
No enterprise messaging platform support (Teams/Slack enterprise)
